title: OTTR
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"fck3t","text":"Organotypic Timelapse recording with Transcriptomic Readout (OTTR) integrates week-long live imaging of sparsely labelled organotypic slice cultures with highly multiplexed in situ spatial transcriptomics. The protocol herein outlines all steps involved in carrying out OTTR, from sample collection to cryosectioning of gelatin-embedded samples.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":173,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4m0ph","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fao0v","text":"In short, acute glioblastoma or fetal cortex samples are:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"emrch","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ap774","text":"- Washed, processed and vibratome-sectioned into 300 um-thick organotypic slices.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"edk5l","text":"- Sparsely labeled with a lentiviral construct, plated onto an insert and cultured for ~3 days.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"87gdo","text":"- Live imaged for an additional week after marker is expressed.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2ag92","text":"- Formaldehyde-fixed, embedded in gelatin and cryosectioned.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dv92m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"adkrc","text":"Familiarize yourself with your institution and country's chemical safety, biosafety and waste disposal guidelines, as well as the risks posited by this protocol. We recommend drafting a risk assessment document and training all personnel accordingly.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1elkp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"clmje","text":"Ensure that all the listed reagents/equipment are available to you and prepare the media/tools in advance, as acute sample collection might be rescheduled or delayed and you must proceed swiftly.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"158f6","text":"Follow the chemical safety, biosafety and waste disposal guidelines from your institution/country. This protocol involves the usage of hazardous volatile chemicals (formaldehyde), biohazardous genetically modified microorganisms (recombinant lentivirus), and sharps (vibratome, cryostat).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3hk0e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1202h","text":"Non-tested human samples also comprise a risk of infection with human pathogens.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Assemble the glass bottom 6-well plate bearing a lentivirally transduced slice onto the microscope chamber and proceed with live imaging as per the detailed protocol in the article.
Pre-warm Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users to 37 °C in a water bath.
Assemble the vibratome bay and place a piece of sticky tape on the block platform.

Make Tissue processing medium Contributed by users by thawing all frozen reagents On ice . Mix as per Table 1 . Reagent Stock Final Volume (for 250 mL) 1 Anti-Anti 100 X 1 X 2.5 mL 2 GlutaMAX 100 X 1 X 2.5 mL 3 DMEM (high glucose, no glutamine) 1 X 242.5 mL 4 HEPES 1 M 10 mM 2.5 mL Table 1. Tissue processing medium composition and suggested volumes.

Collect Sample from the chosen source and keep in pre-chilled Hibernate-E medium during transfer. Ensure that the adequate guidelines are followed during tissue transfer, e.g. usage of a sealed tube within a closed styrofoam box labeled with biological hazard pictograms, protective gear, etc.

Make Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users by thawing all frozen reagents On ice . Mix as per Table 4 . Reagent Stock Final Volume (for 20 mL) 1 Anti-anti 100 X 1 X 200 uL 2 GlutaMAX 100 X 1 X 200 uL 3 N2 supplement 100 X 1 X 200 uL 4 B27 supplement 50 X 1 X 400 uL 5 HEPES 1 M 10 mM 200 uL 6 MEM (no glutamine, phenol red) 1 X 12.54 mL 7 D-glucose 33% - 260 uL 8 HBSS (calcium, magnesium, no phenol red) 5 mL 9 Human serum (heat inactivated, USA origin, sterile filtered) 1 mL Table 4. Slice maintenance medium composition and suggested volumes.

Ensure that all necessary media are prepared beforehand, as per the steps below. Carry out the procedures under sterile conditions, i.e. within a class II biosafety cabinet. To minimise the amount of thawing steps that frozen reagents are subject to, we recommend aliquoting them upon arrival. Use suggested volumes in Table 1 and Table 3 accordingly.

Make Tissue embedding medium Contributed by users by mixing Tissue processing medium Contributed by users and agarose (low melting point) as per Table 2 . %s %s Final agarose percentage chosen varies depending on tissue consistency: Use 2 Mass Percent normally and 1 Mass Percent for very soft tissue samples.
If carbagen access is unavailable next to the vibratome, bubble Tissue processing medium Contributed by users with carbagen for 00:20:00 On ice .
Irradiate stainless steel tools (scalpel, blade, etc.) and vibratome bay under a UV lamp for 00:30:00 . Do so within a class II biosafety cabinet.
Equilibrate air flow in a class II biosafety cabinet habilitated for handling primary human tissue samples.
Place the Sample in a 5 cm-diameter Petri dish with carbonated ice-cold Tissue processing medium Contributed by users on the container
Wash the tissue twice with Tissue processing medium Contributed by users until no traces of blood are left.

Use a scalpel and sterile forceps to remove necrotic areas and blood vessels from the sample. The remaining (viable) tissue should appear elastic and pink. If possible, wear puncture-resistant gloves under the single/double laboratory gloves throughout all steps involving sharp tools.
Introduce a small styrofoam box filled with ice into the laminar flow hood.
Prepare the working area for the initial sample processing:
Pre-warm the Tissue embedding medium Contributed by users by briefly microwaving it until liquid, then keep it at 37 °C in a water bath close to the hood.
Incubate the sample with 1X RBC lysis buffer for 00:10:00 at Room temperature .
Optionally, lyse remaining red blood cells as follows:
Wash the sample twice with Tissue processing medium Contributed by users .
Pour 5-7 mL of pre-warmed Tissue embedding medium Contributed by users in a cryostat plastic embedding mould with an automatic pipettor. Do not introduce any bubbles.
Chill the mould with Tissue embedding medium Contributed by users On ice for 00:01:00 . If it gelates completely, repeat from go to step #12 .
To prevent the tissue strips from floating, sink them to the center and gently swirl them to elute the Tissue processing medium Contributed by users sitting at the interface.
Ensure that the tissue stripes are aligned vertically and well spaced to facilitate vibratome sectioning.
Use forceps to transfer the tissue strips to the mould.
Embed 2-3 tissue strips from go to step #10 :
Transfer the embedded tissue to the premises where the vibratome lies. Wrap the mould in parafilm or otherwise transport it within a sealed sterile bag.
Section 300 um thick slices at a speed of 0.2 mm/s; adjust depending on stiffness.
Use the markings made in go to step #42.4 to find the correct orientation of the cryostat chuck, ensuring good sample alignment with the blade.

Cut open the agarose block and trim excess gelated Tissue embedding medium Contributed by users from all sides. Take all necessary precautions to avoid skin injuries during this step. Wear puncture/cut-resistant gloves, if possible, and firmly hold the block to prevent it slipping away.
Mount the agarose block containing the sample:
Fill the vibratome bay with pre-chilled carbonated Tissue processing medium Contributed by users . If a carbagen outlet is available nearby, keep on bubbling the medium.
Glue the agarose block to the tape and let it dry for 00:10:00 at Room temperature .
Surround the vibratome bay with ice to cool down the Tissue processing medium Contributed by users .
Add 500 µL of Tissue processing medium Contributed by users to every well in 1-2 12-well tissue/cell culture plate(s). Keep them in a styrofoam box filled with ice nearby.
If a perforated spoon is not available and/or the preferred method for capturing vibratome slices, an alternative is to craft a suction tool as follows:

Heat up a glass Pasteur/transfer pipette head atop a Bunsen burner until soft. Follow the safety guidelines for handling Bunsen burners: Do not wear flammable protective equipment, especially gloves; wear heat-resistant gloves, if possible; etc.
Using a set of pliers, cut out the conical section; keep the cylindrical part. Alternatively, saw off the conical section before heating up the pipette head.
Using flat pliers, squeeze the end of the cylinder until oblong. Afterwards, curve it upwards until the whole piece adopts a "hockey stick" shape.
Attach a pipette bulb to the end of the contraption.
In the meantime, set up the following:
When the agarose block is fully adhered to the platform, fill the vibratome bay with Tissue processing medium Contributed by users until the block is fully covered.
Incubate the slices with 15% sucrose in 1X PBS for at least 02:00:00 at 4 °C .
Collect every slice with a perforated spatula or the suction tool ( go to step #1 ). Wiggle it or suck liquid in and out until the slice detaches from the agarose.
Temporarily store the slices in the 12-well tissue/cell culture plate(s) to the side ( go to step #17.3 ).
Upon completion, thoroughly disinfect the vibratome bay with 70% ethanol and proceed to the cell culture room. After the protocol's completion, sterilize the bay with UV before the next usage.
Replace the 15% sucrose solution with 30% sucrose in 1X PBS and incubate Overnight at 4 °C .

Make Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users by dissolving gelatin and sucrose in 1X PBS ( Table 5 ). Reagent Stock Final 1 Type A gelatin from porcine skin 100% 12% (w/v) 2 Sucrose 100% 30% (w/v) 3 PBS 1X 1X Table 5. Gelatin embedding medium. Heating the solution to >50°C will be required for the gelatin to melt and dissolve, after which the solution should be allowed to cool down slightly. We recommend using the embedding medium at ~37°C to ensure it solidifies quickly when applied in the subsequent steps.

Embed the slices (red, Figure 2 ) in Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users . Incubating the submerged tissue slices for 00:30:00 at 37 °C improves tissue adhesion to gelatin, making downstream cryosectioning easier. Allow the gelatin to solidify for 00:30:00 at 4 °C . Figure 2. Embedding of fixed tissue with gelatin.
Cryoprotect the slices before freezing:

Embed the slices for cryosectioning, as per Figure 1 : Figure 1. Overview of the resection protocol, including: a) Sample embedding in 12% gelatin; b) gelatin cap preparation; c) stage trimming; and d) sample protection, re-embedding and assembly onto the stage.

In the meantime, prepare a gelatin stage by applying Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users to a cryostat specimen chuck. Allow the gelatin to solidify for 00:30:00 at 4 °C before freezing on dry ice ( Figure 3 ). Figure 3 . Casting and freezing a gelatin stage. Mount the chuck in the cryostat, mark its orientation for future reference, and cut to make a flat surface ( Figure 4 ). Figure 4. Left) Marking the orientation of the cryostat chuck for downstream alignment; Right) thawed stage after cryosectioning.

Remove the membrane from the culturing insert by making shallow incisions ( Figure 8 ) and peeling the membrane away ( Figure 9 ). Proceed slowly to ensure the integrity of the lowermost layer of cells in the slice. Figure 8. Cutting out a "window" of membrane surrounding the tissue with a scalpel. Figure 9. Peeling away the membrane with tweezers. To reduce friction, and thereby the risk of damaging the tissue slices, we recommend applying 30% sucrose solution in 1X PBS when peeling the membrane or placing the tissue on gelatin in the subsequent steps. Excess solution can later be carefully removed with tissue paper.
Excise the embedded tissue slices from the culturing insert ( Figure 10 ). Figure 10. Excising the tissue-bearing gelatin block with a scalpel.

Prepare a thin gelatin spacer by casting Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users between two parallel flat surfaces (such as two microscope slides; Figure 5 ). Allow the gelatin to solidify for 00:30:00 at 4 °C . The purpose of the spacer is to act as a protective layer for the otherwise exposed tissue slice. Figure 5. Pouring gelatin into an improvised microscope slide mould. Cast the spacer ( Figure 6 ) with the aid of an additional microscope slide. Figure 6. Casting the gelatin spacer by pressing the ensemble with an additional slide. Retrieve the solidified spacer ( Figure 7 ) by detaching the top slide used for casting. Figure 7. Disassembling the slide mould.

Place the embedded tissue slice on the gelatin spacer from go to step #42.5 , with the tissue facing the spacer. Apply a drop of sucrose solution beforehand at the interface and remove the excess with a paper tissue. Figure 11. Stacking the tissue block onto the protective spacer and trimming the excess gelatin.

Place the re-embedded tissue slice ( go to step #42.9 ) onto the flat gelatin stage ( go to step #42.4 ), as per Figure 13 . Apply a drop of sucrose solution at the interface beforehand and remove the excess liquids with a paper tissue. Figure 13. Stacking the re-embedded ensemble onto the trimmed gelatin stage.

Allow the sample to equilibrate to the desired temperature. We recommend colder temperature settings to ensure smoother handling of cryosections, as gelatin will be stickier than O.C.T. (in our experience, temperatures of around -24 °C works best). For the same stickiness reason, we recommend not using anti-roll glass plates when cryosectioning, as sections may occasionally stick to them. We instead use a paint brush to guide the sections when cutting.

Capture the section of desired thickness on a pre-warmed glass. We recommend using a hot plate to briefly warm-up the slides just before capturing the tissue for better adhesion. While pre-warming is not strictly necessary, we have found that using warm slides makes the section handling easier without any noticeable trade-offs in RNA quality, tissue integrity, etc.

Wash slices twice for 00:10:00 at Room temperature , once in Slice washing medium Contributed by users and another in pre-warmed Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users . Perform the washes in a rocker.
Transfer lentivirus on dry ice and thaw slowly On ice before usage.

Dilute lentivirus in Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users to a final titre of 4000000 Genome copies per ml for glioblastoma slices or 40000000 Genome copies per ml - 70000000 Genome copies per ml for fetal cortex. Pipette up and down carefully. Take all necessary precautions when handling lentiviruses. We recommend double glove usage, as well as employing a class II biosafety cabinet connected to the building's ventilation system.
For each well in the 12-well plate (one at a time), replace all medium with 500 µL of virus-spiked medium per well.
Incubate Overnight in a hypoxic incubator.
The following day, pre-warm Slice washing medium Contributed by users and Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users to 37 °C in a water bath.
Remove virus-spiked medium from each well and perform a quick rinse with 1 mL of Slice washing medium Contributed by users .
Wash twice with 1 mL of Slice washing medium Contributed by users for 00:10:00 at Room temperature while rocking.

Make Slice washing medium Contributed by users by thawing all frozen reagents On ice . Mix as per Table 3 . Reagent Stock Final Volume (for 20 mL) 1 Anti-anti 100 X 1 X 200 uL 2 GlutaMAX 100 X 1 X 200 uL 3 MEM (no glutamine, phenol red) 1 X - 19.60 mL Table 3 . Slice washing medium composition and suggested volumes.
Wash twice with 1 mL of Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users diluted 1/10 in MEM while rocking.
In the meantime, add 1 mL of Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users to any desired number of wells in 6-well tissue/cell culture plates.

Using sterile forceps, lower a PTFE-membrane insert into every well. Keep the plates in the incubator until all washes are completed, or proceed immediately if prepared afterwards. To minimize the risk of bubbles accumulating under the insert's membrane, tilt the plate while depositing the insert and do so slowly and at an angle, so that the medium seeps into the membrane sideways.
Retrieve the plates and, for every insert, carefully deposit a single slice in the middle of it with a sterile perforated spoon or suction tool.
Remove excess medium around the plated tissue until it looks dry. Avoid touching the slice.
Incubate for 48:00:00 in a hypoxic incubator until fluorescent signal is apparent, then transfer to a newly equilibrated 6-well plate with glass bottom.
Change Slice maintenance medium Contributed by users the day after plating, then every 48 hours.

As a reference, the following images illustrate their corresponding substep with food dye-colored Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users , including the tissue itself ( Figure 1 ). Colors are merely indicative, and any implementation should use the media recipe of Table 4 with no modifications. Figure 1. Red-colored gelatin simulating a fixed tissue slice cultured on an insert, immediately after retrieval and fixation.

Apply Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users to bind the re-embedded tissue sample with the stage ( Figure 12 ). Allow the gelatin to solidify for 00:30:00 at 4 °C before freezing on dry ice. Figure 14. Covering the re-embedded ensemble with gelatin to attach it to the stage.

Embed the tissue slice together with the spacer in Gelatin embedding medium Contributed by users between to parallel flat surfaces (such as two microscope slides; Figure 7 ). Allow the gelatin to solidify for 00:30:00 at 4 °C . Figure 12 . Re-embedding the tissue-spacer block within a taller slide mould.
Fix the slices immediately after the timelapse imaging has been completed: Perform tissue fixation within a fume hood connected to the exhaust ventilation system, to prevent formaldehyde inhalation.

Briefly wash the slices with 1X PBS to remove leftover medium. To avoid tissue damage and disruption during washing, we recommend adding most of the solution below the culture insert membrane, with only minimal volume placed atop the membrane, in this and all subsequent steps of sample preparation for embedding.
Fix the slices with 4% formaldehyde in 1X PBS for 00:15:00 at Room temperature .
Wash the slices with 1X PBS at least five times for 00:05:00 each.
